segments.inferno-8-g48: “Rispuose” is an archaic form of “rispose,” the passato remoto, meaning “he replied.” It introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-8-g49: “Vedi che” means “you see that,” with “che” introducing a dependent statement. “Son” is the shortened first-person singular “sono,” and “un che piango” means literally “one who I-weeps,” i.e. “one who weeps”; Dante uses “che” as a relative pronoun referring to “un.”
segments.inferno-8-g50: “E io a lui” is an elliptical narrative formula: the verb of speaking is omitted but understood, “And I said to him.” “A lui” marks the addressee of Dante’s reply.
segments.inferno-8-g51: “Con piangere e con lutto” uses “con” twice for emphasis: “with weeping and with mourning/grief.” “Piangere” is an infinitive used as a noun, while “lutto” adds the idea of sorrowful mourning.
segments.inferno-8-g52: “Spirito maladetto” is a vocative, directly addressing the damned soul. “Maladetto” is an older or poetic form of “maledetto,” meaning “accursed.”
segments.inferno-8-g53: “Ti rimani” is an imperative-like command: “remain yourself” or “stay where you are.” The reflexive “ti” gives the verb a force like “stay put” or “remain there.”
segments.inferno-8-g54: “Ch’i’” is a contraction of “ché io,” meaning “for/because I.” “Ti conosco” means “I know/recognize you,” with “ti” as the direct object before the present-tense verb “conosco.”
segments.inferno-8-g55: “Ancor sie lordo tutto” is concessive: “though you be wholly filthy.” “Sie” is an archaic subjunctive form of “essere,” and “tutto” functions adverbially, meaning “entirely/completely.”
segments.inferno-8-g56: “Allor” marks the next action, “then.” “Distese” is passato remoto, “he stretched out,” and “al legno” means “toward/to the boat,” since “legno” poetically means a vessel; “ambo le mani” means “both hands.”
segments.inferno-8-g57: “Per che” means “wherefore/whereupon,” linking Virgil’s reaction to the spirit’s reaching for the boat. “’L maestro accorto” is “the prudent/alert master,” and “lo sospinse” means “pushed him back/away,” with “lo” as the object pronoun.
segments.inferno-8-g58: “Dicendo” is a gerund, “saying,” attached to the main action “lo sospinse.” It introduces the words spoken while or as he pushed the spirit away.
segments.inferno-8-g59: “Via” is used interjectionally here, meaning “away!” rather than literally “road” or “way.” “Costà” points dismissively to a place over there, and “con li altri cani” means “with the other dogs,” with “li” an older masculine plural article.
segments.inferno-8-g60: This target contains only punctuation and no grammatical words to analyze.
segments.inferno-8-g37: Mentre introduces a temporal subordinate clause, “while.” Corravam is an archaic/poetic form for correvamo, imperfect indicative first-person plural, used here transitively with la morta gora: “we were coursing/rushing across the dead, stagnant water.” Morta functions as an adjective, “dead/stagnant,” rather than as the finite verb “died.”
segments.inferno-8-g38: Dinanzi mi means “in front of me,” with mi as an indirect/dative pronoun of reference. Mi si fece is a reflexive idiom: literally “made himself to me,” meaning “presented himself/appeared before me.” Un pien di fango is elliptical for “someone/a person full of mud,” with pien a shortened poetic form of pieno.
segments.inferno-8-g39: E links this action to the appearance in the previous segment. Disse is passato remoto, the normal narrative past tense in Dante, meaning “he said.”
segments.inferno-8-g40: Chi se’ tu means “Who are you?”; se’ is the apocopated poetic form of sei. Che is a relative pronoun referring back to tu, “who,” and vieni is present indicative. Anzi ora means “before the time/hour,” i.e. before the moment when this living person should be here among the dead.
segments.inferno-8-g41: This segment contains only punctuation, closing the preceding direct speech; there are no words to analyze.
segments.inferno-8-g42: E continues the exchange, and io is the explicit subject, emphasized before the implied verb of speaking. A lui means “to him”; the verb “said/replied” is omitted but understood from the dialogue structure.
segments.inferno-8-g43: S’i’ is the contraction of se io, “if I”; the supplied form S’ is not reflexive here but the conditional conjunction se elided before i’. Vegno is an archaic/poetic first-person singular present form for vengo, “I come.”
segments.inferno-8-g44: Non negates the present-tense verb rimango, “I remain/stay.” The clause answers the mud-covered speaker’s question: Dante says that although he comes here, he is not remaining among the damned.
segments.inferno-8-g45: Ma marks a contrast and turns the questioning back on the speaker. Tu is placed before chi se’ for emphasis: “but you, who are you?” Se’ again is the shortened poetic form of sei, “you are.”
segments.inferno-8-g46: Che is a relative pronoun referring to tu: “who.” Sì is an intensifying adverb, “so,” and se’ fatto is a construction with essere plus past participle meaning “you have become/been made.” Brutto here means not simply ugly but physically foul or filthy, because the figure is covered in mud.
segments.inferno-8-g47: This segment contains only punctuation, closing Dante’s direct speech; there are no words to analyze.
segments.inferno-8-g23: “Flegïàs” is a direct vocative address to Phlegyas, the ferryman. The diaeresis marks the pronunciation across syllables in the poem.
segments.inferno-8-g24: The repeated “Flegïàs” intensifies the address and arrests his angry shouting. It is still vocative, not a grammatical subject.
segments.inferno-8-g25: “Tu gridi” is present indicative, second-person singular: Virgil tells Phlegyas that he is shouting. “A vòto” is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning “in vain,” with “vòto” literally connected with emptiness.
segments.inferno-8-g26: “Disse” is the passato remoto of “dire,” the standard narrative past tense in Dante. “Lo mio segnore” means “my lord/master”; the article before the possessive, “lo mio,” is normal in older Italian.
segments.inferno-8-g27: “A questa volta” means “on/for this occasion” or “this time.” “Volta” here has the temporal sense “time,” not a physical turning or vault.
segments.inferno-8-g28: “Più non ... che” forms a limiting comparison: “not more than,” hence “only as much as.” “Ci avrai” means “you will have us,” with “ci” as direct object; “passando il loto” is a gerund phrase meaning “while crossing the mud/mire.”
segments.inferno-8-g29: “Qual è colui che...” opens a simile: literally, “as is he who...” or “like the man who....” “Che li sia fatto” is a dependent clause with subjunctive “sia” and passive “fatto,” meaning that the trick has been done to him; “li” is archaic for dative “gli,” “to him.”
segments.inferno-8-g30: “E poi” continues the comparison: after hearing of the trick, the person reacts emotionally. “Se ne rammarca” is a pronominal construction: “se” is reflexive and “ne” means “about it/of it,” giving “grieves over it” or “laments it.”
segments.inferno-8-g31: “Fecesi” is an archaic enclitic form, “si fece,” here meaning “became” or “showed himself to be.” “Ne l’ira accolta” means “in gathered/pent-up anger,” with “accolta” describing wrath collected within him.
segments.inferno-8-g32: “Lo duca mio” means “my guide,” again with the older article-before-possessive pattern. “Discese ne la barca” uses passato remoto “discese” and “ne la” for modern “nella,” here with motion into the boat.
segments.inferno-8-g33: “Mi fece intrare” is a causative construction: literally “he made me enter,” meaning “he had me enter” or “caused me to enter.” “Appresso lui” means “after him” or “behind him,” though the provided lemma for “appresso” does not match this use.
segments.inferno-8-g34: “Sol quand’ io fui dentro” means “only when I was inside”; “quand’” is elided before “io.” “Parve carca” means “it seemed laden,” referring to the boat, which only appears heavy once Dante, a living body, is aboard.
segments.inferno-8-g35: “Tosto che” is a conjunction meaning “as soon as.” “’l” is the elided article “il,” and “legno,” literally “wood,” is a poetic word for a boat or vessel; “fui” is singular in form even though the sense includes both the guide and Dante.
segments.inferno-8-g36: “Segando” is a gerund meaning “cutting through,” describing how the prow moves through the water. “Se ne va” is a pronominal form of “andare,” “goes off/proceeds”; “più che non suol” is a comparison, where “non” is pleonastic after “più che” and does not create a real negative.
segments.inferno-8-g61: The normal prose order would be “poi mi cinse lo collo con le braccia.” “Lo collo” is the direct object, while “mi” functions with a possessive force, meaning “my neck”; “cinse” is a past definite form of “cingere,” here describing an embrace or encircling with the arms.
segments.inferno-8-g62: “Basciommi” is an archaic fused form of “basciò mi,” meaning “he kissed me”; the verb is past definite, continuing the sequence of actions. “’l volto” means “the face,” with the article elided, and “e disse” introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-8-g63: “Alma sdegnosa” is a vocative, directly addressing Dante as a “soul” characterized by righteous indignation or disdain. The adjective follows the noun in normal poetic order and carries a positive moral tone here.
segments.inferno-8-g64: “Benedetta colei” means “blessed is she,” with the verb “is” understood. “Che ’n te s’incinse” means “who became pregnant with you / conceived you”: “’n” is a contraction of “in,” and “s’incinse” is a past definite reflexive form used in the sense of becoming pregnant.
segments.inferno-8-g65: “Quei” is a poetic demonstrative meaning “that man,” referring to the damned soul just mentioned. “Fu” is the past definite of “essere,” and “al mondo” means “in earthly life,” not merely at a physical location.
segments.inferno-8-g66: The clause means literally “there is no goodness that adorns his memory.” “È” has existential force, “there is,” and “fregi” is a present subjunctive verb form of “fregiare,” required after the negative indefinite idea “no goodness that...”.
segments.inferno-8-g67: “Così” draws the conclusion: because no virtue honors his memory, his shade is correspondingly furious here. “S’è” is an archaic or poetic reflexive construction with “essere,” close in sense to “is/shows itself,” and “l’ombra sua” means “his shade,” that is, his soul in Hell.
segments.inferno-8-g68: “Quanti” is exclamatory: “How many!” “Si tegnon” is an archaic shortened form of “si tengono,” meaning “consider themselves”; “là sù” means “up there,” referring to the living world above, while “staranno” is future tense, pointing to their eventual condition in Hell.
segments.inferno-8-g69: “Lasciando” is a gerund, expressing the result or accompanying circumstance: they will leave behind a hateful memory of themselves. “Di sé” means “of themselves,” and “dispregi” here means disgrace or contempt attached to their reputation.
segments.inferno-8-g70: This target contains only punctuation and no grammatical words to analyze.
segments.inferno-8-g12: This target segment contains only punctuation; there are no Italian words or grammatical constructions to analyze.
segments.inferno-8-g13: “Ed elli a me” is an elliptical speech tag: the verb “disse” is omitted but understood, so literally it is “And he to me.” “Elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” meaning “he,” not related here to the listed noun senses.
segments.inferno-8-g14: “Su per” works as a compound directional expression, meaning over or across the surface of the waves. “Scorgere puoi” uses poetic inversion of the normal order “puoi scorgere,” and “quello che s’aspetta” means “that which is awaited,” with “s’” functioning as a passive/impersonal marker.
segments.inferno-8-g15: “Se” is the ordinary conjunction “if,” though that sense is not among the listed options. “’l fummo del pantan” means “the smoke/fog of the marsh,” where “fummo” is an archaic spelling of “fumo”; “nol” contracts “non lo,” so the clause means “if it does not conceal it from you.”
segments.inferno-8-g16: This is the first half of a comparison: “Corda non ... mai” means “Never did a bowstring...” “Pinse” is a poetic/archaic form meaning “drove, pushed, launched,” and “corresse” is an imperfect subjunctive used after the comparative/result construction “sì ... che”; “via” here is adverbial, meaning “away/off.”
segments.inferno-8-g17: “Com’ io vidi” completes the comparison begun in the previous segment: “as I saw.” “Vidi ... venir” is a perception verb followed by an infinitive, literally “I saw a little boat come,” and “in quella” is an elliptical phrase meaning “at that moment/then.”
segments.inferno-8-g18: “Sotto ’l governo” means “under the guidance/control,” with “’l” contracted from “il.” “D’un sol galeoto” means “of/by a single boatman”; “sol” is the apocopated form of “solo,” and “galeoto” here means the one managing the boat.
segments.inferno-8-g19: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “galeoto,” the boatman. “Gridava” is imperfect indicative, describing an ongoing action: he was shouting or crying out as the boat approached.
segments.inferno-8-g20: “Or” means “now,” and “se’” is the shortened poetic form of “sei,” “you are.” “Giunta” is a feminine singular past participle agreeing with the addressed “anima,” so the phrase means “Now you have arrived.”
segments.inferno-8-g21: “Anima fella” is a vocative phrase: the boatman directly addresses the newcomer as a “wicked soul.” “Fella” is a feminine adjective agreeing with “anima.”
segments.inferno-8-g22: This target segment contains only punctuation; there are no Italian words or grammatical constructions to analyze.
segments.inferno-8-g1: “Io dico” is the narrator’s first-person present: Dante resumes the act of telling the story. The explicit pronoun “Io” is emphatic in Italian, though English normally does not need extra stress.
segments.inferno-8-g2: “Seguitando” is a gerund meaning “continuing,” attached parenthetically to “Io dico.” It signals that Dante is continuing the narrative from the previous canto or episode.
segments.inferno-8-g3: “Ch’” introduces the content of what Dante says. “Assai prima che” means “long before”; “fossimo” is imperfect subjunctive after the temporal conjunction “prima che,” and “al piè de l’alta torre” means “at the foot/base of the tall tower,” with “de l’” for modern “dell’.” The supplied senses for “torre” do not include the noun “tower,” so its sense is marked -1.
segments.inferno-8-g4: The subject is “li occhi nostri,” an archaic form for “i nostri occhi,” but the meaning is closer to “our gaze.” “N’andar” is a poetic past-tense form for “se n’andarono/andarono,” with “n’” giving the sense of movement away/upward; “suso a la cima” means “up to the top.” “Che i vedemmo porre” means “which we saw being set there”: “i” here functions as an archaic locative “there,” not as the article listed in the senses.
segments.inferno-8-g5: This phrase coordinates with the previous object of perception: they saw another flame “da lungi,” from far away, “render cenno,” return or give a sign. “Un’altra” means “another one,” referring back to “fiammetta/fiamma.”
segments.inferno-8-g6: “Tanto ch’” introduces a result clause: “so much so that.” “A pena” is an idiom meaning “scarcely” or “with difficulty,” and “potea” is a poetic imperfect form of “poteva.” The word order is inverted: literally, “scarcely could the eye take it away,” meaning the gaze could hardly withdraw itself from the sight.
segments.inferno-8-g7: “Mi volsi” is a reflexive past form: “I turned myself,” naturally “I turned.” “Al mar di tutto ’l senno” is a metaphor for Virgil, “the sea of all wisdom”; “’l” is the shortened form of “il.”
segments.inferno-8-g8: “Dissi” is the first-person singular passato remoto, the narrative past tense commonly used in Dante: “I said.” It introduces the direct question that follows.
segments.inferno-8-g9: “Questo” refers to the visible signal just described. “Che dice?” is literally “what does it say?” but in context means “what does it mean?” because the flames function as signals.
segments.inferno-8-g10: This continues Dante’s direct question. “Che” is interrogative “what,” while “quell’altro foco” means “that other fire/flame,” referring to the answering signal seen from afar.
segments.inferno-8-g11: This is the final coordinated question in Dante’s speech. “Son” is the shortened form of “sono,” and “quei che” means “those who.” “’L” is not the article here but an object pronoun, “it,” referring to the signal; “fenno” is an archaic form of “fecero,” “they made/did.”
segments.inferno-8-g98: “Ed” links this reply to the preceding speech, and “ei” is an archaic poetic form for “he,” referring to the guide. “Mi disse” uses the passato remoto of “dire,” with “mi” as an indirect object: “said to me.”
segments.inferno-8-g99: “Il foco etterno” is the subject, with the archaic spelling “foco” for modern “fuoco” and “etterno” for “eterno.” The relative clause “ch’entro l’affoca” means “which burns/inflames it within”; the elided “l’” is pronominal here rather than the article listed. “Le dimostra rosse” has “le” as “them,” referring back to the mosques/towers, and “dimostra” means “shows/makes appear.”
segments.inferno-8-g100: “Come” introduces a comparison: “as you see.” “Tu vedi” is present indicative, and “in questo basso inferno” locates the sight in this “lower” region of Hell, with “basso” used spatially and morally in the infernal geography.
segments.inferno-8-g101: “Noi pur giugnemmo” uses “pur” in the sense of “still / at last / nevertheless,” and “giugnemmo” is an archaic passato remoto form of “giungere,” “we arrived.” “Dentro a l’alte fosse” means “inside to the deep ditches”; “alte” can poetically mean “deep” when applied to trenches. “Che vallan quella terra sconsolata” is a relative clause, “which surround that desolate city,” where “terra” means a town or city rather than the earth generally.
segments.inferno-8-g102: “Le mura” is the subject, meaning the city walls. “Mi parean” is an apocopated imperfect form for “mi parevano,” “seemed to me.” The clause “che ferro fosse” uses imperfect subjunctive “fosse” after “parean”: literally, “that it was iron,” naturally “that they were made of iron.”
segments.inferno-8-g103: “Non sanza” is the older spelling of “non senza,” “not without”; the supplied lemma for “sanza” is not relevant here. “Prima far” uses the infinitive after the prepositional idea “before,” and “grande aggirata” means a large circling round or wide detour.
segments.inferno-8-g104: “Venimmo” is passato remoto, “we came/reached,” and “in parte” means “to a place.” “Dove” introduces the place where the action occurs. “Il nocchier forte” is the boatman, described as strong or mighty, and “Usciteci” is an imperative plural form, “come/get out here,” with “ci” attached as a locative particle.
segments.inferno-8-g105: “Gridò” is passato remoto, marking the boatman’s sudden completed cry. The colon introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-8-g106: “Qui” points to the exact spot reached by the boat. “È” is present indicative of “essere,” and “l’intrata” is an archaic form of “entrata,” meaning “the entrance.”
segments.inferno-8-g107: “Io vidi” is passato remoto: “I saw.” “Più di mille” means “more than a thousand,” with the noun omitted but understood as spirits or beings. “In su le porte” means “up on/upon the gates,” and “da ciel piovuti” is a past participial phrase, literally “rained down from heaven,” meaning fallen from heaven.
segments.inferno-8-g108: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to the more than a thousand beings just seen: “who.” “Stizzosamente” modifies “dicean,” and “dicean” is an apocopated imperfect form of “dicevano,” giving the sense “were saying” or “kept saying.”
segments.inferno-8-g109: “Chi è costui” is a direct question: “Who is this man/fellow?” The relative clause “che sanza morte va” means “who goes without death,” that is, while still alive. “Per lo regno de la morta gente” means “through the realm of the dead people”; “de la” is the older separated form of modern “della,” and “morta” is adjectival here, not the verb sense listed.
segments.inferno-8-g82: The semicolon marks that this line continues the preceding action rather than beginning an independent narrative unit. It separates the crowd’s cry from the result that follows.
segments.inferno-8-g83: “e ’l” is the contraction of “e il,” with the article before “fiorentino spirito bizzarro.” The subject is “the irascible Florentine spirit,” and “si volvea” is an imperfect reflexive/medio-passive form, meaning he was turning himself or turning upon himself; “in sé medesmo” intensifies “upon his very self,” while “co’ denti” means “with the teeth,” i.e. by biting.
segments.inferno-8-g84: “Quivi” means “there,” referring to the place where Filippo Argenti is left behind. “il” is not the article here but an object pronoun meaning “him,” and “lasciammo” is first-person plural preterite: “we left.”
segments.inferno-8-g85: “che” introduces an explanatory clause, here roughly “for/because,” though that exact sense is not in the list. “più non” has the sense “no more / no longer,” and “ne” means “about him/about it,” referring back to the preceding episode; “narro” is present tense, “I tell/recount.”
segments.inferno-8-g86: “ma” turns from the abandoned sinner to a new sound that draws Dante’s attention. “ne l’orecchie” is the older separated form of “nelle orecchie,” literally “in the ears”; “mi percosse” uses “mi” as an indirect/dative pronoun, “struck me in the ears,” and “un duolo” is a lament or cry of pain.
segments.inferno-8-g87: “per ch’io” is “per che io,” meaning “wherefore/so that I,” giving the result of the lament striking his ears. The word order is poetic: the object phrase “l’occhio intento” comes before the verb “sbarro,” while “avante” modifies the action, “forward”; “sbarro” means “I open wide,” especially of the eyes or gaze.
segments.inferno-8-g88: “Lo buon maestro” is an archaic form for “il buon maestro,” with “lo” used as the definite article before the noun phrase. “disse” is the preterite of “dire,” introducing direct speech.
segments.inferno-8-g89: “Omai” means “now” or “by now,” marking that the travelers have reached a new stage of the journey. It stands at the beginning of Virgil’s speech for emphasis.
segments.inferno-8-g90: “figliuolo” is a vocative, “my son/child,” addressed affectionately by Virgil to Dante. The possessive is understood rather than expressed.
segments.inferno-8-g91: “s’appressa” is “si appressa,” a pronominal verb meaning “draws near,” with the city as the grammatical subject. “c’ha” is a contraction of “che ha,” “which has,” so “c’ha nome Dite” means “which has the name Dis” or “which is called Dis.”
segments.inferno-8-g92: “coi” contracts “con i,” meaning “with the.” “cittadin” is a shortened poetic plural form for “cittadini,” and “gravi” characterizes them as grave, weighty, or grievous in tone.
segments.inferno-8-g93: “col” contracts “con il,” parallel to the preceding “coi.” “grande stuolo” means a large host or troop, completing the description of Dis as populated by a multitude.
segments.inferno-8-g94: “E io” is an elliptical speech tag: the verb “said” is understood from the dialogue context. This compact formula means “And I [said].”
segments.inferno-8-g95: “Maestro” is a vocative addressed to Virgil. Dante uses the title rather than the name, emphasizing Virgil’s role as guide and teacher.
segments.inferno-8-g96: “già” means “already,” and the object “le sue meschite” precedes the verb “cerno” in poetic order. “là entro” means “there within,” “ne la valle” is the older separated spelling of “nella valle,” and “certe” gives the sense of the mosques being clearly or unmistakably discerned.
segments.inferno-8-g97: “vermiglie” is a feminine plural adjective agreeing with “meschite,” describing them as red or vermilion. “come se” introduces a hypothetical comparison requiring the subjunctive: “fossero uscite” is a pluperfect subjunctive compound, “had come out,” and “di foco” means “from fire.”
segments.inferno-8-g71: This is an elliptical speech tag: “And I,” with a verb such as “said” understood from context. The subject pronoun io is explicit for emphasis and to mark the change of speaker.
segments.inferno-8-g72: Maestro is a vocative, directly addressing Virgil. It stands outside the syntax of the following clause and functions like “Master,” or “Teacher.”
segments.inferno-8-g73: sarei is conditional, expressing Dante’s wish politely: “I would be.” vago di takes an infinitive complement, “desirous/eager to,” and vederlo combines vedere with the object pronoun lo, “to see him.” prima che introduces a temporal subordinate clause; uscissimo is imperfect subjunctive, required after prima che for an anticipated action: “before we should leave.”
segments.inferno-8-g74: This is another elliptical speech tag: “And he to me,” meaning “And he said to me.” elli is an archaic form for egli, “he,” though the supplied lemma does not reflect that usage.
segments.inferno-8-g75: Avante che means “before,” introducing a temporal clause. la proda is the subject, and ti si lasci veder is a reflexive/middle construction: literally, “lets itself be seen to you,” meaning “comes into your sight.” lasci is subjunctive because it depends on the temporal conjunction avante che.
segments.inferno-8-g76: tu sarai sazio is a future-tense main clause: “you will be satisfied.” sazio is a predicate adjective agreeing with tu, and it refers to the satisfaction of Dante’s desire just expressed.
segments.inferno-8-g77: convien che means “it is necessary/fitting that,” and it governs the subjunctive goda. di tal disïo belongs with goda: literally “enjoy of such a desire,” i.e. “have the enjoyment/satisfaction of such a desire.”
segments.inferno-8-g78: Dopo ciò poco has poetic word order for poco dopo ciò, “a little after that.” vid’ is apocopated vidi, “I saw,” and the subject io follows the verb for meter and emphasis. The infinitive far depends on vidi; quello strazio is its object, di costui specifies the person mangled, and a le fangose genti marks the agent/subject of the infinitive in an old causative-style construction.
segments.inferno-8-g79: che introduces a result clause: the sight was such “that” Dante still praises and thanks God for it. Dio is placed before the verbs as their object, while ne is a clitic meaning “for it/about it,” referring back to the event just described. lodo and ringrazio are first-person singular present verbs.
segments.inferno-8-g80: Tutti is the plural subject, “all/everyone.” gridavano is imperfect indicative, describing an ongoing or repeated shout in the scene: “were shouting.”
segments.inferno-8-g81: A Filippo Argenti is an elliptical cry, with a understood sense like “at him!” or “against Filippo Argenti!” The preposition a marks the target of the shouted attack, and Filippo Argenti is a proper name.
segments.inferno-8-g139: “Così” refers back to Virgil’s promise and action: “thus” or “in this way.” “Sen va” is the poetic contraction of “se ne va,” literally “he goes away from there,” with present tense used narratively.
segments.inferno-8-g140: “Quivi” means “there,” marking the place where Dante is left. The object pronoun “m’” is elided before the vowel of “abbandona,” while the subject “lo dolce padre” follows the verb in poetic order; “lo” is an archaic form of the masculine article.
segments.inferno-8-g141: “Rimagno” is an archaic or poetic form of modern “rimango,” first-person present of “rimanere,” here meaning “I remain” or “am left.” The phrase “in forse” is idiomatic: literally “in perhaps,” meaning in uncertainty or doubt.
segments.inferno-8-g142: Here “che” introduces the reason or explanation for Dante’s doubt. “Sì e no” are personified as opposing answers, and “mi tenciona” means “contend/quarrel within me”; “nel capo” gives the mental location of this inner debate.
segments.inferno-8-g143: The infinitive “Udir” is placed first for emphasis: “to hear, I could not.” “Potti” is an old preterite form of “potere,” meaning “I could,” and “quello ch’a lor porse” means “that which he offered/presented to them,” with “a lor” as the indirect object.
segments.inferno-8-g144: “Ei” is the subject pronoun “he,” referring to Virgil. “Stette” is a preterite of “stare,” here “stayed,” and “guari” is an archaic adverb meaning “long” or “much,” though it is not the modern adjective meaning “healed.”
segments.inferno-8-g145: The “che” has a consecutive sense after “non stette ... guari”: he did not stay long before this happened. “Dentro” gives the direction “back inside,” “a pruova” is an idiom meaning competitively or in rivalry, and “si ricorse” is a reflexive-looking preterite form meaning “ran back.”
segments.inferno-8-g146: “Chiuser” is an apocopated poetic form of “chiusero,” third-person plural preterite: “they closed.” The subject “que’ nostri avversari” follows the verb, and “nel petto al mio segnor” is an idiom meaning “in the breast/front of my lord,” that is, right in his face.
segments.inferno-8-g147: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to “mio segnor,” Virgil. “Fuor” means “outside,” and “rivolsesi” is “si rivolse” with the reflexive pronoun attached after the preterite verb, a common poetic order; “passi rari” literally means “sparse steps,” hence slow, measured steps.
segments.inferno-8-g148: The subject is still Virgil, carried over from the previous sentence. “Li occhi a la terra” is elliptical, meaning that he had his eyes directed toward the ground; “avea rase” governs “le ciglia,” with “rase” agreeing in feminine plural and meaning “shorn/stripped” of all “baldanza,” or confidence and boldness.
segments.inferno-8-g149: “Dicea” is the imperfect of “dire,” here “he was saying” or “he said,” giving an ongoing, subdued action. “Ne’” is the contracted form of “nei,” from “in + i,” so “ne’ sospiri” means “in/among his sighs.”
segments.inferno-8-g150: “Chi” begins an exclamatory question: “Who has...!” The pronoun “m’” is dative, “to me,” and “ha negate” is a present perfect construction; “negate” agrees with the feminine plural object “le dolenti case,” meaning the painful or sorrowful dwellings of the infernal city.
segments.inferno-8-g120: “Pensa” is a second-person singular imperative from pensare, directly addressing the reader: “think” or “consider.” It introduces an indirect question beginning with “se” in the next segment.
segments.inferno-8-g121: “Lettor” is the apocopated poetic form of “lettore,” “reader.” It is a vocative, set off by commas, identifying the person being addressed by the imperative “Pensa.”
segments.inferno-8-g122: Here “se” means “whether/if,” introducing the indirect question governed by “Pensa.” “Io mi sconfortai” uses the passato remoto with the reflexive clitic “mi,” meaning “I lost heart”; “nel suon de le parole maladette” means “at/in the sound of the accursed words,” with archaic “de le” for modern “delle.”
segments.inferno-8-g123: “Ché” gives the reason: “for/because.” “Credetti” is passato remoto, “I believed/thought,” followed by the infinitive “ritornarci,” where “ci” means “there”; with “non ... mai,” the sense is “I did not ever believe,” i.e. “I never thought.”
segments.inferno-8-g124: This is a vocative address to Virgil. “O” is an exclamation, “caro” modifies “duca,” and “mio” follows the noun in normal Italian possessive placement for emphasis: “O my dear guide.”
segments.inferno-8-g125: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring back to “duca mio”: “who.” The coordinated perfects “m’hai ... renduta” and “hai ... tratto” mean “you have restored to me” and “you have rescued/drawn me out,” while “d’alto periglio” means “from deep/grave peril”; the final relative clause “che ’ncontra mi stette” describes the peril as one that “stood against me,” with “’ncontra” an archaic form related to “incontra/contro.”
segments.inferno-8-g126: “Non mi lasciar” is a negative command expressed with the infinitive “lasciar,” a common Italian construction: “do not leave me.” The clitic “mi” is the object of “lasciar.”
segments.inferno-8-g127: “Diss’ io” is “dissi io,” with elision before “io”: “said I.” The inverted order is poetic and also marks the interruption of Dante’s quoted speech by a reporting clause.
segments.inferno-8-g128: “Così disfatto” completes the plea “non mi lasciar”: “do not leave me so undone.” “Disfatto” is a past participle used adjectivally, meaning emotionally broken or overwhelmed.
segments.inferno-8-g129: “E se” introduces a conditional clause, “and if.” “’l passar più oltre” uses the substantivized infinitive “passar,” literally “the passing farther,” and “ci è negato” is a passive construction meaning “is denied to us.”
segments.inferno-8-g130: “Ritroviam” is an apocopated first-person plural form, equivalent to “ritroviamo,” used hortatively: “let us find again/retrace.” “L’orme nostre” means “our tracks/footprints,” and “insieme ratto” adds “together, quickly.”
segments.inferno-8-g131: “Quel segnor” refers to Virgil with a respectful title, “that lord/master.” The relative clause “che lì m’avea menato” means “who had led me there”; “avea” is the imperfect form of “avere” used as an auxiliary with the past participle “menato.”
segments.inferno-8-g132: “Mi disse” means “said to me,” with “mi” as an indirect object clitic. The verb “disse” is passato remoto, the normal narrative past tense in Dante.
segments.inferno-8-g133: “Non temer” is a negative imperative using the infinitive “temer,” an apocopated form of “temere.” It means “do not fear.”
segments.inferno-8-g134: “Ché” introduces the explanation, “for/because.” “’l nostro passo” means “our passage,” and the order “non ci può tòrre alcun” is poetic: literally, “not from us can take any one,” meaning “no one can take it from us.”
segments.inferno-8-g135: “Da tal” means “by such a one,” reverently alluding to a higher divine authority without naming it directly. “N’è dato” is a passive construction, literally “it is given to us,” with the elided pronoun functioning as “to us/for us” in sense.
segments.inferno-8-g136: “Ma” marks a contrast with Dante’s plea to turn back: Virgil instead tells him to stay. “Qui m’attendi” places “qui” before the verb for emphasis, and “m’” is the elided object pronoun “me”: “wait for me here.”
segments.inferno-8-g137: The two imperatives “conforta” and “ciba” are addressed to Dante: “comfort/strengthen” and “feed/nourish.” “Lo spirito lasso” is the object of both verbs, and “di speranza buona” supplies what the spirit is to be fed with, literally “of good hope.”
segments.inferno-8-g138: “Ch’i’” contracts “ché io” or “che io,” here giving the reassuring reason: “for I.” “Lascerò” is future first singular, “I will leave,” and “nel mondo basso” means “in the low world,” a phrase for Hell.
segments.inferno-8-g151: This target consists only of a period, marking the end of the preceding speech or sentence. There are no lexical items to analyze in this segment.
segments.inferno-8-g152: “E a me disse” has normal narrative order: conjunction + indirect object + past-tense verb. “Disse” is the passato remoto of “dire,” used for a completed narrative action: “he said.”
segments.inferno-8-g153: “Tu” is an explicit subject pronoun used vocatively and emphatically: Virgil turns directly to Dante. Italian often omits subject pronouns, so its presence gives the command a personal, reassuring force.
segments.inferno-8-g154: “Perch’ io m’adiri” is concessive here: “although/even if I grow angry.” “M’adiri” is a reflexive form of “adirarsi,” with “m’” standing for “mi”; the verb is subjunctive, dependent on the concessive “perch’.”
segments.inferno-8-g155: “Non sbigottir” is a negative imperative with the infinitive form “sbigottir,” an apocopated form of “sbigottire.” The sense is “do not be dismayed” or “do not lose heart,” addressed to “tu.”
segments.inferno-8-g156: “Ch’io vincerò la prova” gives the reason for the command: “for/because I shall overcome the trial.” “Vincerò” is future indicative, expressing Virgil’s confident prediction; “la prova” is the challenge or test posed by the closed gate.
segments.inferno-8-g157: “Qual ch’...” is an indefinite-relative construction meaning “whatever.” “S’aggiri” is subjunctive, matching the indefinite idea: whatever force, plot, or resistance may be stirring inside “a la difension,” that is, in defense against them.
segments.inferno-8-g158: “Questa lor tracotanza” means “this arrogance of theirs,” with “lor” as an archaic possessive form for “their.” “Non è nova” uses “nova,” an old or poetic form of “nuova,” meaning that such insolence is not unprecedented.
segments.inferno-8-g159: “Ché” introduces the explanation: “for/because.” “Già l’usaro” means “they already/once used it,” where “usaro” is an archaic third-person plural passato remoto for “usarono,” and “l’” refers back to their arrogance; “a men segreta porta” means “at a less hidden gate.”
segments.inferno-8-g160: “La qual” is a feminine relative pronoun referring to “porta.” “Sanza” is the older form of “senza,” and “si trova” is a passive-like/reflexive construction meaning “is found” or “still stands”; the gate is still without “serrame,” a fastening or lock.
segments.inferno-8-g161: “Sovr’ essa” means “above it,” with elision of “sovra” before the pronoun. “Vedestù” is a contracted and inverted form of “vedesti tu,” “you saw”; “la scritta morta” is literally “the dead inscription,” referring to the ominous inscription associated with death.
segments.inferno-8-g162: “E già” continues the prophecy with “and already.” “Di qua da lei” is an idiom meaning “on this side of it,” with “lei” referring back to the feminine “porta”; “discende l’erta” is poetic word order, since the actual subject is delayed until “tal” in the final segment.
segments.inferno-8-g163: “Passando” is a gerund, “passing,” describing the manner in which the delayed subject is coming. “Per li cerchi” means “through the circles,” with “li” as an archaic masculine plural article; “sanza scorta” means “without escort.”
segments.inferno-8-g164: “Tal che” introduces the delayed subject: “one such that.” “Per lui” expresses agency or means, “by/through him”; “fia” is an archaic future form of “essere,” and “ne” here means “to us,” so the sense is that the city will be opened to them by him.
segments.inferno-8-g110: This segment contains only a full stop, marking sentence-ending punctuation; there are no words or grammatical forms to analyze.
segments.inferno-8-g111: “E ’l” is the conjunction plus the contracted article “il,” introducing “il savio mio maestro” as the subject. “Fece segno di voler...” uses the passato remoto “fece” with the idiom “fare segno” = to make/give a sign; “voler” and “parlar” are apocopated infinitives, and “lor” is an indirect object meaning “to them.”
segments.inferno-8-g112: “Allor” links this reaction to the preceding gesture. “Chiusero” is passato remoto, and “chiusero un poco il gran disdegno” is a poetic idiom meaning that they partly shut in or restrained their disdain; “disser” is a shortened form of “dissero,” ‘they said.’
segments.inferno-8-g113: “Vien” is an imperative form of “venire,” addressed to Virgil. The explicit “tu” after the verb is emphatic, and “solo” functions predicatively: ‘you alone.’
segments.inferno-8-g114: “E” coordinates a second command: “quei sen vada” means ‘let that one go away,’ with “quei” as a demonstrative pronoun and “sen” standing for “se ne.” “Vada” is present subjunctive used jussively; the relative clause “che sì ardito intrò...” describes that person as the one who entered the realm so boldly.
segments.inferno-8-g115: “Sol” is fronted for emphasis and means ‘alone.’ “Si ritorni” is a reflexive present subjunctive used as a command or permission, ‘let him return’; “per la folle strada” gives the route, with “folle” characterizing the road as rash or foolish.
segments.inferno-8-g116: “Pruovi” is an archaic spelling of “provi,” present subjunctive third singular. It continues the jussive construction: ‘let him try.’
segments.inferno-8-g117: “Se” here is the conjunction ‘if,’ not a form of ‘essere.’ “Sa” means ‘knows how’ or ‘is able,’ with the relevant infinitive understood from the preceding command to return.
segments.inferno-8-g118: “Ché” is causal, ‘for’ or ‘because,’ introducing the reason the other must go back alone. “Tu” shifts the address back to Virgil, and “rimarrai” is a second-person singular future: ‘you will remain.’
segments.inferno-8-g119: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to “tu.” “Ha’” is an apostrophized form of “hai,” used as the auxiliary with “iscorta”; “li” is not the article here but an archaic pronoun connected with the person being guided, while “sì buia contrada” means ‘so dark a region.’

sentences.inferno-8-s17: He replied: “You see that I am one who weeps.”
sentences.inferno-8-s18: And I to him: “With weeping and with grief, accursed spirit, remain there; for I recognize you, though you are wholly filthy.”
sentences.inferno-8-s19: Then he stretched both hands toward the boat; whereupon the wary master pushed him away, saying: “Away there with the other dogs!”
sentences.inferno-8-s13: While we were rushing across the dead pool, a mud-covered figure appeared in front of me and said, “Who are you, who come before your time?”
sentences.inferno-8-s15: And I said to him: “If I come, I do not stay; but you, who are you, who have become so filthy?”
sentences.inferno-8-s9: “Phlegyas, Phlegyas, you shout in vain,” said my lord, “this time: you shall have us no more than merely while crossing the mire.”
sentences.inferno-8-s10: As one becomes who hears that a great trick has been played on him, and then grieves over it, so Phlegyas became in his pent-up wrath.
sentences.inferno-8-s11: My guide descended into the boat, and then made me enter after him; and only when I was inside did it seem laden.
sentences.inferno-8-s12: As soon as the guide and I were in the vessel, the ancient prow went on, cutting through more of the water than it is wont to do with others.
sentences.inferno-8-s21: Then he encircled my neck with his arms, kissed my face, and said: “Indignant soul, blessed is she who conceived you!”
sentences.inferno-8-s22: That man was an arrogant person in the world; there is no virtue that adorns his memory: therefore his shade is furious here.
sentences.inferno-8-s23: How many now up there consider themselves great kings, who here will stand like pigs in mire, leaving behind horrible disgrace of themselves!”
sentences.inferno-8-s6: And he said to me: “Over the filthy waves you can already discern what is being awaited, if the smoke of the marsh does not conceal it from you.”
sentences.inferno-8-s7: Never did a bowstring launch an arrow from itself that sped so swiftly away through the air as I saw a little boat coming over the water toward us then, under the guidance of a single boatman, who was shouting: “Now you have arrived, wicked soul!”
sentences.inferno-8-s1: I say, continuing, that long before we were at the foot of the tall tower, our eyes went upward to the top because of two little flames that we saw being set there, and another, from afar, returning the signal, so much so that the eye could scarcely tear itself away from it.
sentences.inferno-8-s2: And I turned to the sea of all wisdom; I said, “What does this mean?
sentences.inferno-8-s3: and what does that other flame answer?
sentences.inferno-8-s4: and who are those who made it?”
sentences.inferno-8-s33: And he said to me: “The eternal fire that burns within it makes them appear red, as you see in this lower Hell.”
sentences.inferno-8-s34: Still we came inside to the deep ditches that surround that desolate city; the walls seemed to me to be iron.
sentences.inferno-8-s35: Not without first making a wide circuit, we came to a place where the strong boatman cried, “Get out here; this is the entrance.”
sentences.inferno-8-s36: I saw more than a thousand upon the gates, fallen from heaven, who were angrily saying: “Who is this man who, without death, goes through the realm of the dead people?”
sentences.inferno-8-s29: and the irascible Florentine spirit turned his teeth upon himself.
sentences.inferno-8-s30: There we left him, for I tell no more about him; but a lament struck my ears, so that I open wide my fixed gaze forward.
sentences.inferno-8-s31: The good master said: “Now, my son, the city that is named Dis is drawing near, with its grave citizens, with its great host.”
sentences.inferno-8-s32: And I said: “Master, already I clearly discern its mosques there within the valley, vermilion, as if they had come out of fire.”
sentences.inferno-8-s25: And I said, “Master, I would be very eager to see him plunged into this mire before we leave the lake.”
sentences.inferno-8-s26: And he said to me, “Before the shore lets itself be seen by you, you shall be satisfied: it is fitting that you enjoy such a desire.”
sentences.inferno-8-s27: A little after that, I saw the muddy people make such a mangling of this fellow that even now I praise and thank God for it.
sentences.inferno-8-s45: Thus he goes away, and there my sweet father abandons me, and I remain in doubt, for yes and no contend within my head.
sentences.inferno-8-s46: I could not hear what he offered to them; but he did not stay there with them long, before each one ran back inside in rivalry.
sentences.inferno-8-s47: Those adversaries of ours shut the gates in my lord’s face; he remained outside and turned back to me with slow steps.
sentences.inferno-8-s48: He had his eyes toward the ground and his brows stripped of all boldness, and he said amid his sighs: “Who has denied me the sorrowful houses!”
sentences.inferno-8-s41: Consider, reader, whether I lost heart at the sound of those accursed words, for I did not believe I would ever return there.
sentences.inferno-8-s42: “O my dear guide, who more than seven times have restored assurance to me and rescued me from the deep peril that stood against me, do not leave me,” I said, “so undone; and if going farther is denied us, let us quickly retrace our steps together.”
sentences.inferno-8-s43: And that lord who had led me there said to me: “Do not fear; for no one can take our passage from us: it is granted to us by One such as He.”
sentences.inferno-8-s44: But wait for me here, and comfort and feed your weary spirit with good hope, for I will not leave you in the low world.”
sentences.inferno-8-s50: And he said to me: “You, though I grow angry, do not lose heart, for I shall overcome the trial, whatever may be moving about within for the defense.”
sentences.inferno-8-s51: This arrogance of theirs is not new; for they once used it at a less hidden gate, which still is found without a fastening.
sentences.inferno-8-s52: Above it you saw the dead inscription; and already on this side of it, descending the steep way, passing through the circles without escort, comes one by whom the city will be opened to us.”
sentences.inferno-8-s38: And my wise master made a sign that he wished to speak with them privately.
sentences.inferno-8-s39: Then they restrained somewhat their great disdain and said: “You come alone, and let that one go away, who so boldly entered this realm.
sentences.inferno-8-s40: Let him return alone by the foolish road: let him try, if he knows how; for you shall remain here, you who have escorted him through so dark a region.”
